text: It has become routine to speak of a wave of unarmed and non-violent campaigns against the construction of petrochemical plants producing PX (para-xylene) in Xiamen, Dalian and Ningbo in the past few years. As far as I know, Maoming was the first exception in 2014. Through employing methods such as participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article will focus on three issues, namely that even when the official Chinese media promotes a new form of environmental governance which allegedly enhances citizens' awareness and ability to cope with environment hazards, environmental consciousness and civil resistance can emerge at the grass roots level whether it be because of past experiences with pollution in the same locality or at a distance or due to contagion effect from one campaign to another. The Maoming campaign was much less well organised than previous instances, a factor that possibly contributed to the degree of violence that was involved. In contrast with previous events that triggered extensive coverage from the mass media and drew upon the participation of the urban middle class, this large civil protest was based on social media and the local knowledge of the city and streets of Maoming.
